message.content:
Implement EXACTLY ONE task end-to-end in your isolated git worktree, run the gate, commit on the task branch, return the structured result JSON. Do NOT mutate the ledger; do NOT merge.

Task id: T110 (ledgerRefs goals:G6)
Branch: implement/T110    Base commit: 8d8fa77 (current main HEAD)
headline: Ship the NetworkManager unmanaged-devices drop-in + install.md NM section (C1 + Q40 artifact)
description: Add packaging/networkmanager/99-wanbond-unmanaged.conf containing `[keyfile]\nunmanaged-devices=interface-name:wanbond0`, and (coupled, per AGENTS.md docs-with-code) the C1 install.md §4 NetworkManager subsection: today the docs are networkd-only, but most edge boxes (RPi OS/Debian/Ubuntu desktop) run NM, which flushes the operator's address on link-up without this drop-in (the D39/D5 production failure). Docs state the copy destination (/etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/) and the `nmcli device set`/reload verification step.
acceptance: The drop-in file exists under packaging/ with valid NM keyfile syntax, and a lightweight packaging test/CI check asserts the file's presence and the unmanaged-devices key; install.md §4 gains the NM subsection referencing the shipped file (not a hand-typed inline recipe); validated against an NM host where practical. Relates D39 in acceptance only.

Gate (run via `nix develop -c sh -c '<cmd>'` — go not on bare PATH): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...`. gofmt any Go you add. The "lightweight packaging test/CI check" should be a Go test (e.g. internal or a test/ file) that reads packaging/networkmanager/99-wanbond-unmanaged.conf and asserts it contains the unmanaged-devices=interface-name:wanbond0 key — pick a package that fits the repo layout; look at how existing tests reference repo-root-relative files.

If your worktree is not reachable for edits, `git switch -c t110-fix implement/T110` in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha. Read the existing docs/install.md §4 to match its style. Return {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

content.content:
1	# wanbond — install and operations
2	
3	wanbond ships as a single static binary. One binary serves both roles — the
4	**edge** (the mobile Linux box bonding the WAN uplinks) and the **concentrator**
5	(the public-IP VPS terminating the tunnel); the role is selected by the `role`
6	key in the config file, never by which binary is invoked.
7	
8	> **Provisioning a fresh deployment end-to-end?** Follow the
9	> **[pre-pilot rollout runbook](runbook.md)** — a top-to-bottom operator
10	> procedure (key/PSK generation, both-ends config, standby-concentrator hub
11	> failover, firewall persistence, pacing, and health checks) that ties the
12	> sections below together. This document is the per-topic reference the runbook
13	> points back into.
14	
15	## 1. Build the release binaries
16	
17	From the repo root, inside the dev shell:
18	
19	```sh
20	nix develop -c just release
21	```
22	
23	This cross-compiles `cmd/wanbond` with `CGO_ENABLED=0` (fully static, no libc
24	dependency) for both deployment architectures into `dist/`:
25	
26	- `dist/wanbond-linux-amd64` — x86_64 (typical OCI/AWS VPS concentrator)
27	- `dist/wanbond-linux-arm64` — aarch64 (ARM edge boxes, Ampere VPSes)
28	
29	Verify with `file dist/*`: both must report `statically linked`. Alternatively
30	`nix build` produces the host-architecture binary via the flake package.
31	
32	## 2. Install the binary
33	
34	On each host (pick the artifact matching `uname -m`):
35	
36	```sh
37	install -m 0755 wanbond-linux-<arch> /usr/local/bin/wanbond
38	wanbond version   # prints the stamped build version
39	```
40	
41	## 3. Write the config file — 0600 REQUIRED
42	
43	The daemon takes exactly one flag: `--config <path>`. The systemd units use
44	`/etc/wanbond/edge.toml` and `/etc/wanbond/concentrator.toml`.
45	
46	The file holds the WireGuard private key and the outer-control PSK, so
47	`config.Load` **refuses any file whose permission bits are not exactly `0600`**
48	(`insecure permissions` error at startup). Create it as:
49	
50	```sh
51	mkdir -p /etc/wanbond
52	touch /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
53	chown root:root /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
54	chmod 0600 /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
55	```
56	
57	### Edge config (`/etc/wanbond/edge.toml`)
58	
59	```toml
60	role = "edge"
61	psk = "<base64 32-byte outer-control PSK, same on both ends>"
62	
63	# One [[paths]] block per WAN uplink. source_addr pins the path's UDP socket
64	# to the local source IP the upstream router routes out the intended WAN.
65	[[paths]]
66	name = "starlink"
67	source_addr = "192.168.1.10"
68	
69	[[paths]]
70	name = "5g"
71	source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
72	# dest_addr = "203.0.113.7:51820"  # optional per-path concentrator endpoint;
73	                                   # omit when one public IP fronts all uplinks
74	
75	[wireguard]
76	private_key = "<base64 edge private key>"
77	
78	[[wireguard.peers]]
79	public_key = "<base64 concentrator public key>"
80	endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"     # required on the edge
81	# endpoints = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]  # ordered hub-failover
82	# form (Q18): first entry is the active/primary concentrator, the rest are
83	# ordered standbys tried in order when the active one is lost. Mutually
84	# exclusive with `endpoint` above — `endpoint` is just its one-element form,
85	# so a single-concentrator deployment keeps using it unchanged. Edge-only.
86	# Hub failover (all-paths-down detection + switch/re-handshake to the next
87	# endpoint) is implemented: T54 config surface + T57 switch.
88	allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]     # concentrator's inner tunnel address
89	
90	[metrics]
91	listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"          # loopback only; the daemon rejects anything else
92	
93	[log]
94	level = "info"
95	```
96	
97	### Concentrator config (`/etc/wanbond/concentrator.toml`)
98	
99	```toml
100	role = "concentrator"
101	psk = "<same PSK as the edge>"
102	
103	# The concentrator learns the edge's real per-path endpoints from
104	# authenticated traffic; it only needs its own bind address.
105	[[paths]]
106	name = "wan0"
107	source_addr = "10.0.0.5"           # the VPS's primary (private) interface IP
108	
109	[wireguard]
110	private_key = "<base64 concentrator private key>"
111	listen_port = 51820                # required on the concentrator
112	
113	[[wireguard.peers]]
114	public_key = "<base64 edge public key>"
115	allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.2/32"]     # edge's inner tunnel address; no endpoint —
116	                                   # the concentrator roams the edge dynamically
117	
118	[metrics]
119	listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"
120	
121	[log]
122	level = "info"
123	```
124	
125	Optional on both ends: an `[amnezia]` obfuscation block (all-or-nothing — when
126	used, `jc`, `jmin`, `jmax`, `s1`, `s2` must all be set, identically on both
127	ends). Omit it entirely for plain WireGuard framing.
128	
129	Generate keys with standard WireGuard tooling (`wg genkey | tee k | wg pubkey`)
130	and the PSK with `head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64`.
131	
132	### Optional `[fec]` forward-error-correction plane
133	
134	FEC is **off** unless an `[fec]` block is present. A fixed-ratio block protects
135	each group of `data_shards` (K) inner datagrams with `parity_shards` (M) parity
136	frames at a constant M/K overhead:
137	
138	```toml
139	[fec]
140	enabled = true
141	data_shards = 10
142	parity_shards = 6        # in adaptive mode this is the parity CEILING
143	# adaptive = true        # opt into the closed-loop controller (below)
144	# target_residual = 0.005  # residual-loss SLA — the recommended adaptive surface
145	# safety_factor = 4.0    # legacy headroom multiplier (mutually exclusive with target_residual)
146	```
147	
148	With `adaptive = true` the send side runs the loss-tracking controller: the
149	per-group parity floats in `[0, parity_shards]` to match measured path loss, so
150	a clean path spends near-zero overhead. Two mutually-exclusive ways size that
151	parity — **set exactly one**:
152	
153	- **`target_residual`** (recommended, the primary surface): the target
154	  **post-recovery residual-loss** fraction in `(0,1)`. The controller derives the
155	  minimum parity M whose modeled binomial residual `E[max(0,D-M)]/K`
156	  (`D ~ Bin(K, smoothed loss)`) is at/below this target for the current loss and
157	  K, capped at the `parity_shards` ceiling. It maps an operator's loss budget
158	  directly to redundancy: e.g. `target_residual = 0.005` holds the post-recovery
159	  loss at/below 0.5% (the P4 bound) as long as the ceiling allows. Raising the
160	  ceiling (`parity_shards`) lets a tighter target be met under heavier loss.
161	- **`safety_factor`** (legacy): a bare headroom multiplier ≥ 1 sizing M so
162	  `M/(K+M) ≥ safety_factor × loss`. It does **not** map to a residual bound — an
163	  operator must hand-tune it per (loss, K) to clear a given SLA (the reason
164	  `target_residual` supersedes it). Defaults to 1.5 when adaptive and neither
165	  field is set; note 1.5 sizes M=1 at 5% loss with K=10 (~1% residual), so a
166	  sub-1% SLA needs a higher factor **or**, preferably, `target_residual`.
167	
168	Setting both `target_residual` and `safety_factor` is rejected at config load.
169	
170	### Optional `[scheduler]` weighted aggregation + pacing
171	
172	The send scheduler defaults to **active-backup** (one active path, instant
173	failover). An optional `[scheduler]` block selects the **weighted-aggregation**
174	policy and, off by default, per-path send-**pacing** (token buckets that bound
175	bufferbloat under sustained load):
176	
177	```toml
178	[scheduler]
179	policy = "weighted"
180	pacing_enabled = true     # OFF by default; when on, size the pace from the links below
181	```
182	
183	When pacing is enabled you may declare each uplink's bandwidth and baseline RTT
184	directly on its `[[paths]]` block; the daemon then sizes the per-path pace from the
185	bandwidth-delay product at config load, instead of the synthetic default:
186	
187	```toml
188	[[paths]]
189	name = "starlink"
190	source_addr = "192.168.1.10"
191	link_bandwidth = "50Mbit"  # SI bit/s: k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9 (e.g. "10Mbit", "1Gbit")
192	link_rtt = "45ms"          # baseline RTT — the delay term of the pacing burst
193	```
194	
195	- The declaration is **operator-declared, not auto-tuned**: the value is fixed at
196	  load; wanbond does not adjust it live. Measure it once per link.
197	- It is **all-or-nothing**: declare `link_bandwidth` (and `link_rtt`) on *every*
198	  path or none. The shared per-path pace is sized to the **slowest declared link**
199	  (the bottleneck), so a partial declaration is rejected.
200	- A declared bandwidth with `pacing_enabled = false` (the default) is **inert** —
201	  the synthetic default pace is kept and the tunnel behaves as before.
202	- `link_bandwidth` is **mutually exclusive** with the raw `per_path_capacity_fps` /
203	  `pacing_burst_frames` knobs: declare the link bandwidth *or* set the frame-slot
204	  knobs, not both. A non-positive or unparseable bandwidth/RTT is rejected at load.
205	
206	### Optional `[dns]` resolver block
207	
208	Hostname peer endpoints (opt-in per peer with `dns = true`, see the endpoint
209	list note above) are resolved through the OS **system resolver by default** —
210	an absent `[dns]` block is inert. To route that resolution through a private
211	DNS-over-HTTPS or DNS-over-TLS resolver instead, add an explicit `[dns]`
212	block:
213	
214	```toml
215	[dns]
216	resolver = "doh"                                # "system" (default) | "doh" | "dot"
217	doh_url = "https://198.51.100.1/dns-query"       # required iff resolver = "doh"
218	# dot_server = "198.51.100.1"                    # required iff resolver = "dot"
219	# poll_interval = "30s"                          # re-resolution cadence; must be > 0
220	# timeout = "5s"                                 # per-lookup bound; must be > 0
221	```
222	
223	- `[dns]` only **selects the transport** the per-peer `dns = true` opt-in uses;
224	  it never turns hostname resolution on by itself.
225	- **BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant**: `doh_url`/`dot_server`'s host must itself be
226	  reachable *without* a DNS lookup (otherwise resolving your private
227	  resolver's own name would need the very system resolver you configured it
228	  to avoid). Give it as an IP literal, or set `bootstrap_ip` explicitly when
229	  it is a hostname — config load fails fast otherwise. `bootstrap_ip` must
230	  stay absent when the host is already an IP literal; a non-empty value
231	  there is rejected at load as a mode mismatch.
232	- `dot_server` dials the fixed IANA-assigned DoT port (853); an explicit
233	  `host:port` form must use that exact port.
234	- `doh_url`/`dot_server` are mode-specific: setting the wrong one for the
235	  selected `resolver` (or either one under `resolver = "system"`) is
236	  rejected at load.
237	- **Tolerant boot**: a hostname endpoint that cannot be resolved at startup
238	  (resolver down, DNS outage) never fails bring-up — the tunnel comes up
239	  *without* that peer endpoint and the background re-resolution loop installs
240	  it and initiates the handshake on the first successful lookup. Steady-state
241	  re-resolution then repoints the bond whenever the record changes.
242	
243	### 3a. Tuning per-link bandwidth and pacing
244	
245	**Pacing ships DISABLED by default.** When enabled with `pacing_enabled = true`
246	under the `[scheduler]` block, wanbond sizes the per-path send-pace from the
247	bandwidth-delay product (BDP) — the product of each uplink's usable bandwidth and
248	round-trip latency — to bound bufferbloat (excessive queueing) under sustained load.
249	The declared bandwidth is **operator-measured, not auto-tuned**: you measure it once
250	per link and enter it in the config.
251	
252	This section describes how to measure the required values (`link_bandwidth` and
253	`link_rtt`), where to enter them, and how to verify pacing is effective.
254	
255	#### Step 1: Measure baseline (idle) round-trip time
256	
257	Measure the idle RTT on each path — the latency with light traffic:
258	
259	```sh
260	# From the edge, ping the concentrator's tunnel address (e.g., 10.77.0.1).
261	# Use -c 10 for a quick sample; ignore the first ping (ARP/cold cache).
262	ping -c 10 10.77.0.1
263	```
264	
265	Example output:
266	```
267	round-trip min/avg/max/stddev = 20.1/21.5/23.2/1.0 ms
268	```
269	
270	Record the **average** RTT (here: 21.5 ms). If RTTs are highly variable, the path
271	is jittery; take a longer sample (e.g. `-c 30`). Each path's idle RTT becomes the
272	`link_rtt` value in the config. If paths have different RTTs, declare each path's
273	own value separately.
274	
275	#### Step 2: Measure usable bandwidth per uplink
276	
277	You have two options:
278	
279	##### Option A: Measurement via the capped-fixture test (T52, netns)
280	
281	If you have access to a lab setup (or are developing/testing wanbond), the test
282	suite includes a deterministic bandwidth-measurement sub-test. From the repo root:
283	
284	```sh
285	# This runs the entire fixture-impairment suite, including the BDP sub-test:
286	go test -tags e2e -run TestFixtureImpairment -v ./test/e2e
287	```
288	
289	Toward the end of the output, you will see a `bdp` sub-test log (the numbers below
290	are **illustrative placeholders** — the sub-test is report-only and its measured
291	values vary run to run; read the actual figures from *your* run's log):
292	
293	```
294	path capped BDP: idle RTT=<e.g. ~5>ms loaded RTT=...ms (bufferbloat Δ=...ms) | 
295	  achieved throughput=<e.g. ~35-56> Mbit/s | BDP=... bytes (...frames @ ≈1540B/frame) | 
296	  SizePacingFromBDP -> capacityFPS=... burstFrames=...
297	```
298	
299	The **achieved throughput** (whatever your run reports) is a measured point;
300	the fixture builds a sustained queue by running iperf3 under a controlled bandwidth
301	cap, so it measures the true link-limited throughput (not CPU-bound). The per-frame
302	size (`≈1540B`) is the full path MTU (1500) plus the outer-frame DATA overhead
303	(40) that `SizePacingFromBDP` uses to convert bandwidth to a frame rate.
304	
305	##### Option B: Measurement on the real deployment (manual)
306	
307	Measure each uplink independently with iperf3, one at a time:
308	
309	```sh
310	# On the concentrator, start the iperf3 server:
311	iperf3 -s -B 10.77.0.1
312	
313	# On the edge, run a sustained transfer to build a standing queue (8–10 seconds):
314	# This measures the throughput the link will sustain under sustained load
315	# and allows RTT to stabilize under queue pressure.
316	iperf3 -c 10.77.0.1 -t 10
317	```
318	
319	Example output:
320	```
321	Bitrate         Jitter  Lost/Total Datagrams
322	...
323	  50.2 Mbit/s  ...     ...
324	```
325	
326	If using TCP (default), read the throughput from the final summary line.
327	
328	**Important:** Repeat this measurement for each uplink **separately** — bring up
329	only one path at a time, or isolate it at the router layer. The measurement must
330	reflect each link's independent capacity, not the bonded throughput.
331	
332	Also measure the RTT under load (as a sanity check on bufferbloat):
333	
334	```sh
335	# While iperf3 is running (in another terminal on the edge):
336	ping -i 0.2 10.77.0.1
337	```
338	
339	Record the average RTT under load. If it is much higher than the idle RTT
340	(e.g. idle 20 ms → loaded 200 ms), the path has severe bufferbloat; pacing will
341	help control this (that is the whole point).
342	
343	#### Step 3: Enter the measured values in the config
344	
345	For each path, add `link_bandwidth` and `link_rtt` to the `[[paths]]` block:
346	
347	```toml
348	[[paths]]
349	name = "starlink"
350	source_addr = "192.168.1.10"
351	link_bandwidth = "50Mbit"    # from Step 2 measurement: 50.2 Mbit/s → round to 50Mbit
352	link_rtt = "21ms"            # from Step 1: 21.5 ms idle RTT → round to 21ms
353	
354	[[paths]]
355	name = "5g"
356	source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
357	link_bandwidth = "10Mbit"    # measured as slower
358	link_rtt = "45ms"            # higher baseline latency
359	```
360	
361	**Rules:**
362	- **Declare on every path or none.** If you declare `link_bandwidth` on one path,
363	  you must declare it on all (wanbond sizes pacing to the slowest link, the
364	  bottleneck). Partial declarations are rejected at load.
365	- **Round conservatively.** Round down if unsure (e.g., measure 49.8 Mbit/s →
366	  declare `49Mbit` rather than `50Mbit`). Under-sized pacing is safe; over-sized
367	  pacing may not bind and bufferbloat may occur.
368	- **Use the `link_rtt` from Step 1**, not the loaded RTT from Step 2. The idle RTT
369	  is the baseline delay the BDP calculation assumes; the loaded RTT tells you how
370	  much bufferbloat exists.
371	
372	#### Step 4: Enable pacing and deploy
373	
374	Ensure the edge config has the weighted scheduler and pacing enabled:
375	
376	```toml
377	[scheduler]
378	policy = "weighted"
379	pacing_enabled = true
380	```
381	
382	Reload or restart the daemon:
383	
384	```sh
385	systemctl reload wanbond-edge   # SIGHUP: re-reads config, applies path changes
386	# or
387	systemctl restart wanbond-edge  # full restart if config is invalid
388	```
389	
390	Verify there are no errors in the journal:
391	
392	```sh
393	journalctl -u wanbond-edge -n 20
394	```
395	
396	A successful load will log `config loaded` (or, on reload, `config reloaded`);
397	if the daemon rejects the config (e.g. inconsistent bandwidth declarations,
398	unparseable values), the error message will say why.
399	
400	#### Step 5: Verify bufferbloat is controlled
401	
402	While running a sustained load (iperf3 from edge to concentrator), measure the
403	RTT under pacing:
404	
405	```sh
406	# Edge, run iperf3 traffic:
407	iperf3 -c 10.77.0.1 -t 30
408	
409	# Edge, another terminal, sample RTT under load:
410	ping -i 0.2 10.77.0.1
411	```
412	
413	If pacing is working, the RTT under sustained load should be **close to the idle
414	RTT** (ideally within 5–10 ms, depending on the link's buffering). Before pacing,
415	you may have seen loaded RTT inflate to 100+ ms on a bufferbloated link; pacing
416	bounds the queue so the inflation is minimal.
417	
418	**Note:** the test suite's netns fixture is CPU-bound and does not build the
419	standing queues needed to validate pacing against real links. Real-link
420	verification (this step) is essential: measure on your actual uplinks to confirm
421	pacing meets your bufferbloat target.
422	
423	### 3z. Full configuration reference (all keys)
424	
425	This is the exhaustive key list — every configuration key wanbond reads, in one
426	place. The example below is **edge-oriented**; concentrator-only keys are shown
427	and marked `CONCENTRATOR-ONLY`. Required keys are shown live; optional/defaulted
428	keys are shown **commented-out with their default value**. Uncommenting a key
429	and leaving it at the shown value is a no-op. The per-section notes after the
430	example capture the cross-field constraints a single example cannot express
431	inline. All of these are enforced at config load (`config.Load`), which fails
432	fast on the first violation — except the loopback and `allowed_ips` checks,
433	noted below.
434	
435	> The same content ships as a copy-pasteable file at the repo root,
436	> [`wanbond.example.toml`](../wanbond.example.toml): copy it, fill the
437	> `<placeholders>`, and delete what you do not need.
438	
439	```toml
440	# ── top level ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
441	role = "edge"                      # REQUIRED. "edge" | "concentrator". Never
442	                                   #   inferred from other keys.
443	psk  = "<base64 32-byte PSK>"      # REQUIRED. 32 raw bytes, base64. Same value
444	                                   #   on both ends; keys the PSK-HMAC that
445	                                   #   authenticates outer PROBE/CONTROL frames.
446	
447	# ── paths: one [[paths]] block per WAN uplink; at least one is REQUIRED ───────
448	[[paths]]
449	name        = "starlink"           # REQUIRED. Stable, unique identifier.
450	source_addr = "192.168.1.10"       # REQUIRED. Bare local source IP the path's
451	                                   #   UDP socket binds to. Must be unique across
452	                                   #   paths (a shared source collides EADDRINUSE
453	                                   #   at the second bind). No default.
454	# dest_addr = "203.0.113.7:51820"  # OPTIONAL, edge-only meaning. Per-path
455	                                   #   concentrator endpoint (ip:port). Omit when
456	                                   #   one public IP fronts all uplinks (the
457	                                   #   peer's endpoint is reused). No default.
458	                                   #   Inert on the concentrator (it learns edge
459	                                   #   endpoints from traffic).
460	# link_bandwidth = "50Mbit"        # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared bottleneck
461	                                   #   bandwidth. SI bit/s: k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9
462	                                   #   ("bit" may be written "bps"). Must be > 0.
463	                                   #   Used ONLY under weighted policy + pacing;
464	                                   #   inert otherwise. No default (undeclared).
465	# link_rtt = "45ms"                # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared baseline RTT
466	                                   #   (Go duration). REQUIRED (> 0) when
467	                                   #   link_bandwidth is set under weighted
468	                                   #   pacing; ignored otherwise. No default.
469	
470	# A second uplink (edge). Repeat the block per path.
471	[[paths]]
472	name        = "5g"
473	source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
474	
475	# ── wireguard: inner tunnel key material ─────────────────────────────────────
476	[wireguard]
477	private_key = "<base64 32-byte private key>"  # REQUIRED (both roles).
478	# listen_port = 51820              # CONCENTRATOR-ONLY: REQUIRED (> 0) there;
479	                                   #   omit / leave 0 on the edge. uint16
480	                                   #   (< 1024 needs CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE).
481	
482	# ── wireguard peers: at least one [[wireguard.peers]] is REQUIRED ─────────────
483	[[wireguard.peers]]
484	public_key = "<base64 peer public key>"  # REQUIRED. base64 32-byte key.
485	endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"     # EDGE: REQUIRED (this OR `endpoints`).
486	                                   #   ip:port literal only, no DNS.
487	                                   #   CONCENTRATOR: must be ABSENT (rejected) —
488	                                   #   it roams the edge dynamically. Mutually
489	                                   #   exclusive with `endpoints`.
490	# endpoints = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]
491	                                   # EDGE-ONLY ordered hub-failover list: [0] is
492	                                   #   the active concentrator, the rest ordered
493	                                   #   standbys. ip:port literals only, no DNS;
494	                                   #   duplicates rejected. Mutually exclusive
495	                                   #   with `endpoint` (which is its one-element
496	                                   #   form). Rejected on the concentrator.
497	allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]     # REQUIRED: >= 1 CIDR routed to this peer
498	                                   #   (enforced when the WG UAPI is built).
499	
500	# ── amnezia obfuscation: OPTIONAL, OFF by default (plain WireGuard) ───────────
501	# ALL-OR-NOTHING: either omit the whole block, or set the entire
502	# jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2 set — IDENTICALLY on both ends. One engine per process.
503	# [amnezia]
504	# jc   = 4                         # junk packet count. > 0 when configured.
505	# jmin = 40                        # min junk packet size. > 0; jmin <= jmax.
506	# jmax = 70                        # max junk packet size. > 0.
507	# s1   = 30                        # init-packet junk prefix size. > 0.
508	# s2   = 40                        # response-packet junk prefix size. > 0.
509	                                   #   Constraint: (148 + s1) != (92 + s2) — the
510	                                   #   obfuscated init/response lengths must
511	                                   #   differ. (no defaults: required when block
512	                                   #   present.)
513	# h1 = 1                           # magic header: initiation. Default 1..4 when
514	# h2 = 2                           #   the block is configured but NO header is
515	# h3 = 3                           #   given. When any is set, h1..h4 must be a
516	# h4 = 4                           #   distinct set (values <= 4 mean "standard
517	                                   #   message type").
518	
519	# ── scheduler: OPTIONAL. Omitted => active-backup, all knobs below ignored ────
520	# [scheduler]
521	# policy = "active-backup"         # "active-backup" (DEFAULT) | "weighted".
522	                                   #   Every knob below applies ONLY to
523	                                   #   "weighted"; under active-backup they are
524	                                   #   inert and left unset.
525	# per_path_capacity_fps = 10000.0  # DEFAULT 10000. Reference per-path capacity
526	                                   #   (frame slots/s): aggregation-gate
527	                                   #   denominator and pacing refill rate. > 0.
528	# engage_fraction    = 0.9         # DEFAULT 0.9. Engage aggregation above
529	                                   #   engage_fraction * capacity. In (0, 1].
530	# disengage_fraction = 0.5         # DEFAULT 0.5. Collapse below
531	                                   #   disengage_fraction * capacity. Must be in
532	                                   #   [0, engage_fraction) — the hysteresis band.
533	# collapse_dwell = "2s"            # DEFAULT 2s. Sustained-low dwell before
534	                                   #   collapsing to primary-only. >= 0.
535	# load_tau = "200ms"               # DEFAULT 200ms. Offered-load rate estimator
536	                                   #   time constant. > 0.
537	# pacing_enabled = false           # DEFAULT false. Turn on per-path send-pacing
538	                                   #   (token buckets). Off => buckets bypassed.
539	# pacing_burst_frames = 64.0       # DEFAULT 64. Token-bucket burst (frames).
540	                                   #   > 0 when pacing_enabled.
541	# weight_rtt_floor = "1ms"         # DEFAULT 1ms. RTT floor in the weight
542	                                   #   formula. > 0.
543	# weight_loss_floor = 0.001        # DEFAULT 1e-3. Loss floor under the sqrt in
544	                                   #   the weight formula. > 0.
545	
546	# ── fec: OPTIONAL, OFF by default (no parity on the wire) ─────────────────────
547	# [fec]
548	# enabled = true                   # DEFAULT false. Turns the FEC plane on; when
549	                                   #   false every field below is ignored.
550	# data_shards = 10                 # K: inner datagrams per coding group. >= 1
551	                                   #   when enabled. No default (required).
552	# parity_shards = 6                # M: parity frames per group (and the adaptive
553	                                   #   parity CEILING). >= 1 when enabled;
554	                                   #   K + M <= 256. No default (required).
555	# deadline = "5ms"                 # DEFAULT 5ms. Partial-group flush deadline.
556	                                   #   > 0 and <= 125ms.
557	# adaptive = false                 # DEFAULT false. Closed-loop parity controller
558	                                   #   (parity tracks measured loss up to the
559	                                   #   ceiling). Requires enabled = true.
560	# target_residual = 0.005          # ADAPTIVE-ONLY, primary sizing surface:
561	                                   #   post-recovery residual-loss SLA in (0, 1).
562	                                   #   No default. Mutually exclusive with
563	                                   #   safety_factor.
564	# safety_factor = 1.5              # ADAPTIVE-ONLY, legacy headroom multiplier
565	                                   #   >= 1. DEFAULTS to 1.5 when adaptive and
566	                                   #   NEITHER field is set. Must stay 0 in fixed
567	                                   #   mode. Mutually exclusive with
568	                                   #   target_residual.
569	
570	# ── metrics: OPTIONAL. Omit the block => no /metrics endpoint is served ───────
571	[metrics]
572	listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"          # No default. LOOPBACK-ONLY (127.0.0.0/8,
573	                                   #   ::1, or a hostname resolving only to
574	                                   #   loopback); any other address is REFUSED
575	                                   #   when the endpoint binds (not at load).
576	
577	# ── log: OPTIONAL ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
578	[log]
579	level = "info"                     # DEFAULT "info" (empty => info). One of
580	                                   #   debug | info | warn | error ("warning" is
581	                                   #   accepted for warn). Unknown => fail-fast.
582	```
583	
584	**Cross-field constraints and role applicability (not expressible inline):**
585	
586	- **Roles.** `role` is required and never inferred. Edge-only:
587	  per-peer `endpoint`/`endpoints` (required on the edge, rejected on the
588	  concentrator) and the pacing declarations (`dest_addr` is edge-only in effect,
589	  but is not *rejected* on the concentrator — merely inert). Concentrator-only:
590	  `wireguard.listen_port` (required there, unused on the edge).
591	- **`endpoint` vs `endpoints`.** Mutually exclusive; `endpoint` is the
592	  one-element form of `endpoints`. On the edge exactly one must be present; the
593	  concentrator must set neither. Entries are `ip:port` literals only (no DNS),
594	  de-duplicated.
595	- **Distinct `source_addr`.** Each `[[paths]]` needs a unique, valid
596	  `source_addr` (compared unmapped, so `192.0.2.10` and `::ffff:192.0.2.10`
597	  collide). `name` must also be unique.
598	- **`allowed_ips`.** At least one CIDR per peer; empty is rejected when the WG
599	  configuration is assembled (not by `config.validate`).
600	- **amnezia all-or-nothing.** An unconfigured (all-zero) block is plain
601	  WireGuard. Once *any* of `jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2/h1..h4` is set, the full
602	  `jc,jmin,jmax,s1,s2` set must be `> 0`, `jmin <= jmax`, the init/response
603	  lengths must not collide (`148+s1 != 92+s2`), and `h1..h4` must be a distinct
604	  set (omitted headers default to `1..4`). The same profile must be set on both
605	  ends. One obfuscation engine per process.
606	- **`link_bandwidth` + `link_rtt` are a pair.** Both-or-neither, and
607	  **all-or-nothing across every path**: declare on every `[[paths]]` block or
608	  none (the shared pace is sized to the slowest declared link, undefined under a
609	  partial declaration). They take effect **only** under
610	  `scheduler.policy = "weighted"` with `pacing_enabled = true`; otherwise a
611	  declared bandwidth is inert. When active they are **mutually exclusive** with
612	  the raw `scheduler.per_path_capacity_fps` / `pacing_burst_frames` knobs —
613	  declare link bandwidth *or* set the frame-slot knobs, not both.
614	- **scheduler off-unless-present.** No `[scheduler]` block ⇒ `active-backup` and
615	  every weighted knob ignored. Under `weighted`, omitted knobs take the defaults
616	  shown; the hysteresis band requires `disengage_fraction < engage_fraction`.
617	- **fec off-unless-present.** No `[fec]` block (or `enabled = false`) ⇒ no
618	  parity on the wire. `adaptive = true` requires `enabled = true`. In adaptive
619	  mode set **either** `target_residual` (recommended, `(0,1)`) **or**
620	  `safety_factor` (`>= 1`), never both; both must stay 0 (unset) in fixed mode.
621	- **metrics loopback-only.** `[metrics] listen` must be a loopback address (or a
622	  hostname resolving entirely to loopback); a non-loopback bind is refused when
623	  the endpoint starts. Omit the block to serve no metrics at all.
624	
625	## 4. systemd units
626	
627	Unit files live in `packaging/systemd/`:
628	
629	```sh
630	cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service /etc/systemd/system/          # edge box
631	cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-concentrator.service /etc/systemd/system/  # VPS
632	systemctl daemon-reload
633	systemctl enable --now wanbond-edge      # or wanbond-concentrator
634	```
635	
636	- `systemctl reload wanbond-<role>` sends SIGHUP: the daemon re-reads the
637	  config and applies the **path diff** (add/remove uplinks) without tearing
638	  down the live tunnel. A bad reload is logged and the running config kept.
639	- `Restart=on-failure` restarts the daemon if the tunnel engine tears down
640	  unexpectedly (the process exits non-zero in that case by design).
641	- The units run with `CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN` (TUN creation) plus
642	  standard hardening. If you set `wireguard.listen_port` below 1024, add
643	  `CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE` to the bounding set.
644	
645	### Interface addressing and routing (operator-owned)
646	
647	The daemon creates the TUN interface (`wanbond0`) and brings it
648	administratively **UP** itself (`SIOCSIFFLAGS`/`IFF_UP`) right after creation —
649	so a write to it never silently fails with `EIO` for want of a link-up step —
650	but owns ONLY the tunnel engine: **it never assigns addresses or routes**
651	(wg-quick style, no privileged shell-outs). Assign them with a
652	**systemd-networkd `.network` file**, using the inner addresses from
653	`allowed_ips`:
654	
655	```ini
656	# /etc/systemd/network/10-wanbond0.network  (edge; concentrator: 10.77.0.1/24)
657	[Match]
658	Name=wanbond0
659	
660	[Network]
661	Address=10.77.0.2/24
662	```
663	
664	```sh
665	systemctl enable --now systemd-networkd
666	```
667	
668	Do **not** use a `[Service] ExecStartPost=ip address add … dev wanbond0`
669	drop-in: the units are `Type=exec`, so systemd considers the service started
670	the instant `execve()` returns — *before* the daemon has created `wanbond0` —
671	and the `ExecStartPost` would run against a not-yet-existent interface, fail
672	with "Cannot find device", and (being an un-prefixed `ExecStartPost`) fail the
673	unit and trip the `Restart=on-failure` crash-loop. networkd is race-free: it
674	watches for the interface and applies the address the moment `wanbond0`
675	appears, whenever that is. (The daemon exposes no `sd_notify` readiness, so
676	there is no ordering guarantee to hang an `ExecStartPost` on.)
677	
678	## 5. Firewall
679	
680	### Concentrator: UDP listen port
681	
682	Allow the WireGuard UDP port in from the WAN (and, on OCI, in the subnet's
683	security list / NSG as well — both layers must pass):
684	
685	```sh
686	iptables -I INPUT -p udp --dport 51820 -j ACCEPT
687	```
688	
689	### Concentrator: tunnel-interface ACCEPT — REQUIRED (OCI default-REJECT caveat)
690	
691	The concentrator **must ACCEPT traffic arriving on the wanbond tunnel
692	interface, ordered ahead of any default REJECT rule**:
693	
694	```sh
695	iptables -I INPUT -i wanbond0 -j ACCEPT
696	```
697	
698	Why this is required: Oracle Cloud (OCI) images ship an INPUT chain ending in
699	
700	```
701	-A INPUT -j REJECT --reject-with icmp-host-prohibited
702	```
703	
704	which silently applies to the tunnel interface too. ICMP echo is allowed by an
705	earlier rule, so **ping through the tunnel works while any TCP connection
706	through the tunnel fails with a confusing `No route to host`** (the REJECT's
707	ICMP host-prohibited answer). This exact failure was hit during P0 real-host
708	testing. `iptables -I` (insert at head) places the ACCEPT ahead of the appended
709	(`-A`) REJECT; verify the ordering with `iptables -S INPUT` — the tunnel ACCEPT
710	must appear before the `-j REJECT` line. The same applies to any distro or
711	provider whose default firewall ends INPUT with REJECT/DROP (e.g. firewalld
712	zones): treat "tunnel ACCEPT ahead of the default reject" as a required
713	concentrator deployment step.
714	
715	### Concentrator: persist the rules across reboots — REQUIRED
716	
717	`iptables -I` mutates only the **runtime** chain. On OCI Ubuntu the
718	`netfilter-persistent` boot service **restores `/etc/iptables/rules.v4`**, so a
719	reboot silently discards any runtime-only rule and the tunnel ACCEPT (and the
720	UDP-port ACCEPT) revert to the default REJECT with no signal until
721	re-provisioned (defect D7). The rules **must** therefore be written to the boot
722	rules file. On Debian/Ubuntu:
723	
724	```sh
725	sudo apt-get install -y iptables-persistent   # provides netfilter-persistent
726	sudo netfilter-persistent save                # snapshots the runtime chain to /etc/iptables/rules.v4
727	```
728	
729	(`service iptables save`, or a firewalld permanent rule, on EL.) Verify the
730	tunnel ACCEPT survives by confirming it appears in the saved file:
731	
732	```sh
733	sudo grep -- '-A INPUT -i wanbond0 -j ACCEPT' /etc/iptables/rules.v4
734	```
735	
736	The rule references the tunnel interface by name, so it only matches while
737	`wanbond0` exists; restoring it at boot (before the daemon starts) is harmless.
738	
739	This save is **idempotent** — re-running it against an unchanged chain is a
740	no-op — and the real-host provisioning (`test/realhosts`,
741	`Provision`/`TestRealProvision`, run via `just realhosts-provision`) performs
742	and asserts exactly these steps automatically: it installs
743	`iptables-persistent`, inserts the runtime rule, runs `netfilter-persistent
744	save`, and re-inspects that the rule is present in `/etc/iptables/rules.v4`.
745	
746	### Edge
747	
748	Outbound UDP to the concentrator's `endpoint` (and any per-path `dest_addr`)
749	must be open on every uplink; no inbound rules are needed — the edge initiates.
750	
751	## 6. Observability
752	
753	Each daemon serves Prometheus metrics on the loopback-only `[metrics] listen`
754	address (`curl -s http://127.0.0.1:9090/metrics`). Logs go to stderr →
755	`journalctl -u wanbond-<role>`.
756	
757	## 7. MTU
758	
759	The daemon sets the TUN MTU itself from the bonded-overhead budget (see
760	`docs/p1-mtu.md`); do not override it. If an on-path MTU below the default
761	1500 is in play, see the TCP MSS-clamp guidance in that document.
762	
763	## 8. Limitations
764	
765	### UDP-blocking networks defeat wanbond (no TCP/TLS fallback — by design)
766	
767	wanbond carries every path over **UDP** (WireGuard's transport). Its
768	DPI-resistance goal is that a network which *inspects* traffic cannot
769	distinguish the flow from ordinary UDP: the outer frame codec (amnezia
770	obfuscation + the FEC-framed Bind) removes the WireGuard/VPN fingerprint, so a
771	network doing protocol classification does not identify and block it. This is
772	verified by the `TestP5DPI` / `TestWireFormatAudit` e2e checks (requirement 6):
773	neither nDPI nor Suricata classifies the obfuscated flow as WireGuard or any
774	identified VPN.
775	
776	Obfuscation does **not** help against a network that blocks UDP **wholesale** —
777	dropping all UDP (or all UDP except DNS/QUIC to specific resolvers), regardless
778	of payload. Against such a network wanbond cannot connect, and there is **no
779	in-scope mitigation**:
780	
781	- There is **no TCP or TLS-tunnelled fallback transport**, and adding one is an
782	  **explicit non-goal** for this project. wanbond's value is WAN *bonding* with
783	  adaptive FEC over multiple real uplinks; a single TCP-over-TLS obfuscation
784	  transport (the domain of tools like obfs4/shadowsocks/`udp2raw`) is a
785	  different problem and is deliberately out of scope.
786	- Wholesale-UDP-block is distinct from **DPI classification**: obfuscation
787	  answers the latter (proven), not the former. A hostile network that lets *no*
788	  UDP through cannot be defeated by making the UDP look innocuous.
789	
790	Operationally: if an uplink blocks UDP entirely, treat that uplink as
791	unavailable for wanbond. If **every** uplink blocks UDP, wanbond is not usable
792	on that site; use a different access network or an out-of-scope UDP-encapsulation
793	tool upstream of wanbond. The manual P5 checklist (`docs/manual-checklist.md`)
794	includes a step to confirm this failure mode is understood and, where a test
795	network permits, observed.
796	
797	### DPI port-guessing on WireGuard's registered port 51820 (deployment note)
798	
799	nDPI (and DPI engines generally) will label **any** UDP flow on WireGuard's
800	IANA-registered port **51820** as `WireGuard` / category `VPN` **by a port
801	guess alone — regardless of payload** (nDPI reports this as `Confidence: Match
802	by port`). This is a classification of the *port*, not the *wire format*: a
803	random-payload UDP flow to `:51820` is labelled WireGuard, while the identical
804	payload to a non-registered port is `Unknown`. wanbond's payload is verified
805	indistinguishable from random by `TestWireFormatAudit` (T26) and by
806	`TestP5DPI`, which reads nDPI's per-flow `Confidence` and only treats a
807	**payload/content** match (`Confidence: DPI`) as a classification — a port
808	guess is disregarded.
809	
810	Deployment consequence: `wireguard.listen_port` is operator-configurable, so on
811	a hostile network that classifies by port, **prefer a non-registered UDP port**
812	(any high, unassigned port) for the concentrator's `listen_port` and the edge's
813	`endpoint`. This avoids the trivial port-based "VPN" label. It is a deployment
814	consideration, not a payload weakness — the obfuscated payload itself does not
815	identify the tunnel as WireGuard/VPN.
816

file.content:
# wanbond — install and operations

wanbond ships as a single static binary. One binary serves both roles — the
**edge** (the mobile Linux box bonding the WAN uplinks) and the **concentrator**
(the public-IP VPS terminating the tunnel); the role is selected by the `role`
key in the config file, never by which binary is invoked.

> **Provisioning a fresh deployment end-to-end?** Follow the
> **[pre-pilot rollout runbook](runbook.md)** — a top-to-bottom operator
> procedure (key/PSK generation, both-ends config, standby-concentrator hub
> failover, firewall persistence, pacing, and health checks) that ties the
> sections below together. This document is the per-topic reference the runbook
> points back into.

## 1. Build the release binaries

From the repo root, inside the dev shell:

```sh
nix develop -c just release
```

This cross-compiles `cmd/wanbond` with `CGO_ENABLED=0` (fully static, no libc
dependency) for both deployment architectures into `dist/`:

- `dist/wanbond-linux-amd64` — x86_64 (typical OCI/AWS VPS concentrator)
- `dist/wanbond-linux-arm64` — aarch64 (ARM edge boxes, Ampere VPSes)

Verify with `file dist/*`: both must report `statically linked`. Alternatively
`nix build` produces the host-architecture binary via the flake package.

## 2. Install the binary

On each host (pick the artifact matching `uname -m`):

```sh
install -m 0755 wanbond-linux-<arch> /usr/local/bin/wanbond
wanbond version   # prints the stamped build version
```

## 3. Write the config file — 0600 REQUIRED

The daemon takes exactly one flag: `--config <path>`. The systemd units use
`/etc/wanbond/edge.toml` and `/etc/wanbond/concentrator.toml`.

The file holds the WireGuard private key and the outer-control PSK, so
`config.Load` **refuses any file whose permission bits are not exactly `0600`**
(`insecure permissions` error at startup). Create it as:

```sh
mkdir -p /etc/wanbond
touch /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
chown root:root /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
chmod 0600 /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
```

### Edge config (`/etc/wanbond/edge.toml`)

```toml
role = "edge"
psk = "<base64 32-byte outer-control PSK, same on both ends>"

# One [[paths]] block per WAN uplink. source_addr pins the path's UDP socket
# to the local source IP the upstream router routes out the intended WAN.
[[paths]]
name = "starlink"
source_addr = "192.168.1.10"

[[paths]]
name = "5g"
source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
# dest_addr = "203.0.113.7:51820"  # optional per-path concentrator endpoint;
                                   # omit when one public IP fronts all uplinks

[wireguard]
private_key = "<base64 edge private key>"

[[wireguard.peers]]
public_key = "<base64 concentrator public key>"
endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"     # required on the edge
# endpoints = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]  # ordered hub-failover
# form (Q18): first entry is the active/primary concentrator, the rest are
# ordered standbys tried in order when the active one is lost. Mutually
# exclusive with `endpoint` above — `endpoint` is just its one-element form,
# so a single-concentrator deployment keeps using it unchanged. Edge-only.
# Hub failover (all-paths-down detection + switch/re-handshake to the next
# endpoint) is implemented: T54 config surface + T57 switch.
allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]     # concentrator's inner tunnel address

[metrics]
listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"          # loopback only; the daemon rejects anything else

[log]
level = "info"
```

### Concentrator config (`/etc/wanbond/concentrator.toml`)

```toml
role = "concentrator"
psk = "<same PSK as the edge>"

# The concentrator learns the edge's real per-path endpoints from
# authenticated traffic; it only needs its own bind address.
[[paths]]
name = "wan0"
source_addr = "10.0.0.5"           # the VPS's primary (private) interface IP

[wireguard]
private_key = "<base64 concentrator private key>"
listen_port = 51820                # required on the concentrator

[[wireguard.peers]]
public_key = "<base64 edge public key>"
allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.2/32"]     # edge's inner tunnel address; no endpoint —
                                   # the concentrator roams the edge dynamically

[metrics]
listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"

[log]
level = "info"
```

Optional on both ends: an `[amnezia]` obfuscation block (all-or-nothing — when
used, `jc`, `jmin`, `jmax`, `s1`, `s2` must all be set, identically on both
ends). Omit it entirely for plain WireGuard framing.

Generate keys with standard WireGuard tooling (`wg genkey | tee k | wg pubkey`)
and the PSK with `head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64`.

### Optional `[fec]` forward-error-correction plane

FEC is **off** unless an `[fec]` block is present. A fixed-ratio block protects
each group of `data_shards` (K) inner datagrams with `parity_shards` (M) parity
frames at a constant M/K overhead:

```toml
[fec]
enabled = true
data_shards = 10
parity_shards = 6        # in adaptive mode this is the parity CEILING
# adaptive = true        # opt into the closed-loop controller (below)
# target_residual = 0.005  # residual-loss SLA — the recommended adaptive surface
# safety_factor = 4.0    # legacy headroom multiplier (mutually exclusive with target_residual)
```

With `adaptive = true` the send side runs the loss-tracking controller: the
per-group parity floats in `[0, parity_shards]` to match measured path loss, so
a clean path spends near-zero overhead. Two mutually-exclusive ways size that
parity — **set exactly one**:

- **`target_residual`** (recommended, the primary surface): the target
  **post-recovery residual-loss** fraction in `(0,1)`. The controller derives the
  minimum parity M whose modeled binomial residual `E[max(0,D-M)]/K`
  (`D ~ Bin(K, smoothed loss)`) is at/below this target for the current loss and
  K, capped at the `parity_shards` ceiling. It maps an operator's loss budget
  directly to redundancy: e.g. `target_residual = 0.005` holds the post-recovery
  loss at/below 0.5% (the P4 bound) as long as the ceiling allows. Raising the
  ceiling (`parity_shards`) lets a tighter target be met under heavier loss.
- **`safety_factor`** (legacy): a bare headroom multiplier ≥ 1 sizing M so
  `M/(K+M) ≥ safety_factor × loss`. It does **not** map to a residual bound — an
  operator must hand-tune it per (loss, K) to clear a given SLA (the reason
  `target_residual` supersedes it). Defaults to 1.5 when adaptive and neither
  field is set; note 1.5 sizes M=1 at 5% loss with K=10 (~1% residual), so a
  sub-1% SLA needs a higher factor **or**, preferably, `target_residual`.

Setting both `target_residual` and `safety_factor` is rejected at config load.

### Optional `[scheduler]` weighted aggregation + pacing

The send scheduler defaults to **active-backup** (one active path, instant
failover). An optional `[scheduler]` block selects the **weighted-aggregation**
policy and, off by default, per-path send-**pacing** (token buckets that bound
bufferbloat under sustained load):

```toml
[scheduler]
policy = "weighted"
pacing_enabled = true     # OFF by default; when on, size the pace from the links below
```

When pacing is enabled you may declare each uplink's bandwidth and baseline RTT
directly on its `[[paths]]` block; the daemon then sizes the per-path pace from the
bandwidth-delay product at config load, instead of the synthetic default:

```toml
[[paths]]
name = "starlink"
source_addr = "192.168.1.10"
link_bandwidth = "50Mbit"  # SI bit/s: k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9 (e.g. "10Mbit", "1Gbit")
link_rtt = "45ms"          # baseline RTT — the delay term of the pacing burst
```

- The declaration is **operator-declared, not auto-tuned**: the value is fixed at
  load; wanbond does not adjust it live. Measure it once per link.
- It is **all-or-nothing**: declare `link_bandwidth` (and `link_rtt`) on *every*
  path or none. The shared per-path pace is sized to the **slowest declared link**
  (the bottleneck), so a partial declaration is rejected.
- A declared bandwidth with `pacing_enabled = false` (the default) is **inert** —
  the synthetic default pace is kept and the tunnel behaves as before.
- `link_bandwidth` is **mutually exclusive** with the raw `per_path_capacity_fps` /
  `pacing_burst_frames` knobs: declare the link bandwidth *or* set the frame-slot
  knobs, not both. A non-positive or unparseable bandwidth/RTT is rejected at load.

### Optional `[dns]` resolver block

Hostname peer endpoints (opt-in per peer with `dns = true`, see the endpoint
list note above) are resolved through the OS **system resolver by default** —
an absent `[dns]` block is inert. To route that resolution through a private
DNS-over-HTTPS or DNS-over-TLS resolver instead, add an explicit `[dns]`
block:

```toml
[dns]
resolver = "doh"                                # "system" (default) | "doh" | "dot"
doh_url = "https://198.51.100.1/dns-query"       # required iff resolver = "doh"
# dot_server = "198.51.100.1"                    # required iff resolver = "dot"
# poll_interval = "30s"                          # re-resolution cadence; must be > 0
# timeout = "5s"                                 # per-lookup bound; must be > 0
```

- `[dns]` only **selects the transport** the per-peer `dns = true` opt-in uses;
  it never turns hostname resolution on by itself.
- **BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant**: `doh_url`/`dot_server`'s host must itself be
  reachable *without* a DNS lookup (otherwise resolving your private
  resolver's own name would need the very system resolver you configured it
  to avoid). Give it as an IP literal, or set `bootstrap_ip` explicitly when
  it is a hostname — config load fails fast otherwise. `bootstrap_ip` must
  stay absent when the host is already an IP literal; a non-empty value
  there is rejected at load as a mode mismatch.
- `dot_server` dials the fixed IANA-assigned DoT port (853); an explicit
  `host:port` form must use that exact port.
- `doh_url`/`dot_server` are mode-specific: setting the wrong one for the
  selected `resolver` (or either one under `resolver = "system"`) is
  rejected at load.
- **Tolerant boot**: a hostname endpoint that cannot be resolved at startup
  (resolver down, DNS outage) never fails bring-up — the tunnel comes up
  *without* that peer endpoint and the background re-resolution loop installs
  it and initiates the handshake on the first successful lookup. Steady-state
  re-resolution then repoints the bond whenever the record changes.

### 3a. Tuning per-link bandwidth and pacing

**Pacing ships DISABLED by default.** When enabled with `pacing_enabled = true`
under the `[scheduler]` block, wanbond sizes the per-path send-pace from the
bandwidth-delay product (BDP) — the product of each uplink's usable bandwidth and
round-trip latency — to bound bufferbloat (excessive queueing) under sustained load.
The declared bandwidth is **operator-measured, not auto-tuned**: you measure it once
per link and enter it in the config.

This section describes how to measure the required values (`link_bandwidth` and
`link_rtt`), where to enter them, and how to verify pacing is effective.

#### Step 1: Measure baseline (idle) round-trip time

Measure the idle RTT on each path — the latency with light traffic:

```sh
# From the edge, ping the concentrator's tunnel address (e.g., 10.77.0.1).
# Use -c 10 for a quick sample; ignore the first ping (ARP/cold cache).
ping -c 10 10.77.0.1
```

Example output:
```
round-trip min/avg/max/stddev = 20.1/21.5/23.2/1.0 ms
```

Record the **average** RTT (here: 21.5 ms). If RTTs are highly variable, the path
is jittery; take a longer sample (e.g. `-c 30`). Each path's idle RTT becomes the
`link_rtt` value in the config. If paths have different RTTs, declare each path's
own value separately.

#### Step 2: Measure usable bandwidth per uplink

You have two options:

##### Option A: Measurement via the capped-fixture test (T52, netns)

If you have access to a lab setup (or are developing/testing wanbond), the test
suite includes a deterministic bandwidth-measurement sub-test. From the repo root:

```sh
# This runs the entire fixture-impairment suite, including the BDP sub-test:
go test -tags e2e -run TestFixtureImpairment -v ./test/e2e
```

Toward the end of the output, you will see a `bdp` sub-test log (the numbers below
are **illustrative placeholders** — the sub-test is report-only and its measured
values vary run to run; read the actual figures from *your* run's log):

```
path capped BDP: idle RTT=<e.g. ~5>ms loaded RTT=...ms (bufferbloat Δ=...ms) | 
  achieved throughput=<e.g. ~35-56> Mbit/s | BDP=... bytes (...frames @ ≈1540B/frame) | 
  SizePacingFromBDP -> capacityFPS=... burstFrames=...
```

The **achieved throughput** (whatever your run reports) is a measured point;
the fixture builds a sustained queue by running iperf3 under a controlled bandwidth
cap, so it measures the true link-limited throughput (not CPU-bound). The per-frame
size (`≈1540B`) is the full path MTU (1500) plus the outer-frame DATA overhead
(40) that `SizePacingFromBDP` uses to convert bandwidth to a frame rate.

##### Option B: Measurement on the real deployment (manual)

Measure each uplink independently with iperf3, one at a time:

```sh
# On the concentrator, start the iperf3 server:
iperf3 -s -B 10.77.0.1

# On the edge, run a sustained transfer to build a standing queue (8–10 seconds):
# This measures the throughput the link will sustain under sustained load
# and allows RTT to stabilize under queue pressure.
iperf3 -c 10.77.0.1 -t 10
```

Example output:
```
Bitrate         Jitter  Lost/Total Datagrams
...
  50.2 Mbit/s  ...     ...
```

If using TCP (default), read the throughput from the final summary line.

**Important:** Repeat this measurement for each uplink **separately** — bring up
only one path at a time, or isolate it at the router layer. The measurement must
reflect each link's independent capacity, not the bonded throughput.

Also measure the RTT under load (as a sanity check on bufferbloat):

```sh
# While iperf3 is running (in another terminal on the edge):
ping -i 0.2 10.77.0.1
```

Record the average RTT under load. If it is much higher than the idle RTT
(e.g. idle 20 ms → loaded 200 ms), the path has severe bufferbloat; pacing will
help control this (that is the whole point).

#### Step 3: Enter the measured values in the config

For each path, add `link_bandwidth` and `link_rtt` to the `[[paths]]` block:

```toml
[[paths]]
name = "starlink"
source_addr = "192.168.1.10"
link_bandwidth = "50Mbit"    # from Step 2 measurement: 50.2 Mbit/s → round to 50Mbit
link_rtt = "21ms"            # from Step 1: 21.5 ms idle RTT → round to 21ms

[[paths]]
name = "5g"
source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
link_bandwidth = "10Mbit"    # measured as slower
link_rtt = "45ms"            # higher baseline latency
```

**Rules:**
- **Declare on every path or none.** If you declare `link_bandwidth` on one path,
  you must declare it on all (wanbond sizes pacing to the slowest link, the
  bottleneck). Partial declarations are rejected at load.
- **Round conservatively.** Round down if unsure (e.g., measure 49.8 Mbit/s →
  declare `49Mbit` rather than `50Mbit`). Under-sized pacing is safe; over-sized
  pacing may not bind and bufferbloat may occur.
- **Use the `link_rtt` from Step 1**, not the loaded RTT from Step 2. The idle RTT
  is the baseline delay the BDP calculation assumes; the loaded RTT tells you how
  much bufferbloat exists.

#### Step 4: Enable pacing and deploy

Ensure the edge config has the weighted scheduler and pacing enabled:

```toml
[scheduler]
policy = "weighted"
pacing_enabled = true
```

Reload or restart the daemon:

```sh
systemctl reload wanbond-edge   # SIGHUP: re-reads config, applies path changes
# or
systemctl restart wanbond-edge  # full restart if config is invalid
```

Verify there are no errors in the journal:

```sh
journalctl -u wanbond-edge -n 20
```

A successful load will log `config loaded` (or, on reload, `config reloaded`);
if the daemon rejects the config (e.g. inconsistent bandwidth declarations,
unparseable values), the error message will say why.

#### Step 5: Verify bufferbloat is controlled

While running a sustained load (iperf3 from edge to concentrator), measure the
RTT under pacing:

```sh
# Edge, run iperf3 traffic:
iperf3 -c 10.77.0.1 -t 30

# Edge, another terminal, sample RTT under load:
ping -i 0.2 10.77.0.1
```

If pacing is working, the RTT under sustained load should be **close to the idle
RTT** (ideally within 5–10 ms, depending on the link's buffering). Before pacing,
you may have seen loaded RTT inflate to 100+ ms on a bufferbloated link; pacing
bounds the queue so the inflation is minimal.

**Note:** the test suite's netns fixture is CPU-bound and does not build the
standing queues needed to validate pacing against real links. Real-link
verification (this step) is essential: measure on your actual uplinks to confirm
pacing meets your bufferbloat target.

### 3z. Full configuration reference (all keys)

This is the exhaustive key list — every configuration key wanbond reads, in one
place. The example below is **edge-oriented**; concentrator-only keys are shown
and marked `CONCENTRATOR-ONLY`. Required keys are shown live; optional/defaulted
keys are shown **commented-out with their default value**. Uncommenting a key
and leaving it at the shown value is a no-op. The per-section notes after the
example capture the cross-field constraints a single example cannot express
inline. All of these are enforced at config load (`config.Load`), which fails
fast on the first violation — except the loopback and `allowed_ips` checks,
noted below.

> The same content ships as a copy-pasteable file at the repo root,
> [`wanbond.example.toml`](../wanbond.example.toml): copy it, fill the
> `<placeholders>`, and delete what you do not need.

```toml
# ── top level ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
role = "edge"                      # REQUIRED. "edge" | "concentrator". Never
                                   #   inferred from other keys.
psk  = "<base64 32-byte PSK>"      # REQUIRED. 32 raw bytes, base64. Same value
                                   #   on both ends; keys the PSK-HMAC that
                                   #   authenticates outer PROBE/CONTROL frames.

# ── paths: one [[paths]] block per WAN uplink; at least one is REQUIRED ───────
[[paths]]
name        = "starlink"           # REQUIRED. Stable, unique identifier.
source_addr = "192.168.1.10"       # REQUIRED. Bare local source IP the path's
                                   #   UDP socket binds to. Must be unique across
                                   #   paths (a shared source collides EADDRINUSE
                                   #   at the second bind). No default.
# dest_addr = "203.0.113.7:51820"  # OPTIONAL, edge-only meaning. Per-path
                                   #   concentrator endpoint (ip:port). Omit when
                                   #   one public IP fronts all uplinks (the
                                   #   peer's endpoint is reused). No default.
                                   #   Inert on the concentrator (it learns edge
                                   #   endpoints from traffic).
# link_bandwidth = "50Mbit"        # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared bottleneck
                                   #   bandwidth. SI bit/s: k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9
                                   #   ("bit" may be written "bps"). Must be > 0.
                                   #   Used ONLY under weighted policy + pacing;
                                   #   inert otherwise. No default (undeclared).
# link_rtt = "45ms"                # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared baseline RTT
                                   #   (Go duration). REQUIRED (> 0) when
                                   #   link_bandwidth is set under weighted
                                   #   pacing; ignored otherwise. No default.

# A second uplink (edge). Repeat the block per path.
[[paths]]
name        = "5g"
source_addr = "192.168.2.10"

# ── wireguard: inner tunnel key material ─────────────────────────────────────
[wireguard]
private_key = "<base64 32-byte private key>"  # REQUIRED (both roles).
# listen_port = 51820              # CONCENTRATOR-ONLY: REQUIRED (> 0) there;
                                   #   omit / leave 0 on the edge. uint16
                                   #   (< 1024 needs CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE).

# ── wireguard peers: at least one [[wireguard.peers]] is REQUIRED ─────────────
[[wireguard.peers]]
public_key = "<base64 peer public key>"  # REQUIRED. base64 32-byte key.
endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"     # EDGE: REQUIRED (this OR `endpoints`).
                                   #   ip:port literal only, no DNS.
                                   #   CONCENTRATOR: must be ABSENT (rejected) —
                                   #   it roams the edge dynamically. Mutually
                                   #   exclusive with `endpoints`.
# endpoints = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]
                                   # EDGE-ONLY ordered hub-failover list: [0] is
                                   #   the active concentrator, the rest ordered
                                   #   standbys. ip:port literals only, no DNS;
                                   #   duplicates rejected. Mutually exclusive
                                   #   with `endpoint` (which is its one-element
                                   #   form). Rejected on the concentrator.
allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]     # REQUIRED: >= 1 CIDR routed to this peer
                                   #   (enforced when the WG UAPI is built).

# ── amnezia obfuscation: OPTIONAL, OFF by default (plain WireGuard) ───────────
# ALL-OR-NOTHING: either omit the whole block, or set the entire
# jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2 set — IDENTICALLY on both ends. One engine per process.
# [amnezia]
# jc   = 4                         # junk packet count. > 0 when configured.
# jmin = 40                        # min junk packet size. > 0; jmin <= jmax.
# jmax = 70                        # max junk packet size. > 0.
# s1   = 30                        # init-packet junk prefix size. > 0.
# s2   = 40                        # response-packet junk prefix size. > 0.
                                   #   Constraint: (148 + s1) != (92 + s2) — the
                                   #   obfuscated init/response lengths must
                                   #   differ. (no defaults: required when block
                                   #   present.)
# h1 = 1                           # magic header: initiation. Default 1..4 when
# h2 = 2                           #   the block is configured but NO header is
# h3 = 3                           #   given. When any is set, h1..h4 must be a
# h4 = 4                           #   distinct set (values <= 4 mean "standard
                                   #   message type").

# ── scheduler: OPTIONAL. Omitted => active-backup, all knobs below ignored ────
# [scheduler]
# policy = "active-backup"         # "active-backup" (DEFAULT) | "weighted".
                                   #   Every knob below applies ONLY to
                                   #   "weighted"; under active-backup they are
                                   #   inert and left unset.
# per_path_capacity_fps = 10000.0  # DEFAULT 10000. Reference per-path capacity
                                   #   (frame slots/s): aggregation-gate
                                   #   denominator and pacing refill rate. > 0.
# engage_fraction    = 0.9         # DEFAULT 0.9. Engage aggregation above
                                   #   engage_fraction * capacity. In (0, 1].
# disengage_fraction = 0.5         # DEFAULT 0.5. Collapse below
                                   #   disengage_fraction * capacity. Must be in
                                   #   [0, engage_fraction) — the hysteresis band.
# collapse_dwell = "2s"            # DEFAULT 2s. Sustained-low dwell before
                                   #   collapsing to primary-only. >= 0.
# load_tau = "200ms"               # DEFAULT 200ms. Offered-load rate estimator
                                   #   time constant. > 0.
# pacing_enabled = false           # DEFAULT false. Turn on per-path send-pacing
                                   #   (token buckets). Off => buckets bypassed.
# pacing_burst_frames = 64.0       # DEFAULT 64. Token-bucket burst (frames).
                                   #   > 0 when pacing_enabled.
# weight_rtt_floor = "1ms"         # DEFAULT 1ms. RTT floor in the weight
                                   #   formula. > 0.
# weight_loss_floor = 0.001        # DEFAULT 1e-3. Loss floor under the sqrt in
                                   #   the weight formula. > 0.

# ── fec: OPTIONAL, OFF by default (no parity on the wire) ─────────────────────
# [fec]
# enabled = true                   # DEFAULT false. Turns the FEC plane on; when
                                   #   false every field below is ignored.
# data_shards = 10                 # K: inner datagrams per coding group. >= 1
                                   #   when enabled. No default (required).
# parity_shards = 6                # M: parity frames per group (and the adaptive
                                   #   parity CEILING). >= 1 when enabled;
                                   #   K + M <= 256. No default (required).
# deadline = "5ms"                 # DEFAULT 5ms. Partial-group flush deadline.
                                   #   > 0 and <= 125ms.
# adaptive = false                 # DEFAULT false. Closed-loop parity controller
                                   #   (parity tracks measured loss up to the
                                   #   ceiling). Requires enabled = true.
# target_residual = 0.005          # ADAPTIVE-ONLY, primary sizing surface:
                                   #   post-recovery residual-loss SLA in (0, 1).
                                   #   No default. Mutually exclusive with
                                   #   safety_factor.
# safety_factor = 1.5              # ADAPTIVE-ONLY, legacy headroom multiplier
                                   #   >= 1. DEFAULTS to 1.5 when adaptive and
                                   #   NEITHER field is set. Must stay 0 in fixed
                                   #   mode. Mutually exclusive with
                                   #   target_residual.

# ── metrics: OPTIONAL. Omit the block => no /metrics endpoint is served ───────
[metrics]
listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"          # No default. LOOPBACK-ONLY (127.0.0.0/8,
                                   #   ::1, or a hostname resolving only to
                                   #   loopback); any other address is REFUSED
                                   #   when the endpoint binds (not at load).

# ── log: OPTIONAL ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
[log]
level = "info"                     # DEFAULT "info" (empty => info). One of
                                   #   debug | info | warn | error ("warning" is
                                   #   accepted for warn). Unknown => fail-fast.
```

**Cross-field constraints and role applicability (not expressible inline):**

- **Roles.** `role` is required and never inferred. Edge-only:
  per-peer `endpoint`/`endpoints` (required on the edge, rejected on the
  concentrator) and the pacing declarations (`dest_addr` is edge-only in effect,
  but is not *rejected* on the concentrator — merely inert). Concentrator-only:
  `wireguard.listen_port` (required there, unused on the edge).
- **`endpoint` vs `endpoints`.** Mutually exclusive; `endpoint` is the
  one-element form of `endpoints`. On the edge exactly one must be present; the
  concentrator must set neither. Entries are `ip:port` literals only (no DNS),
  de-duplicated.
- **Distinct `source_addr`.** Each `[[paths]]` needs a unique, valid
  `source_addr` (compared unmapped, so `192.0.2.10` and `::ffff:192.0.2.10`
  collide). `name` must also be unique.
- **`allowed_ips`.** At least one CIDR per peer; empty is rejected when the WG
  configuration is assembled (not by `config.validate`).
- **amnezia all-or-nothing.** An unconfigured (all-zero) block is plain
  WireGuard. Once *any* of `jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2/h1..h4` is set, the full
  `jc,jmin,jmax,s1,s2` set must be `> 0`, `jmin <= jmax`, the init/response
  lengths must not collide (`148+s1 != 92+s2`), and `h1..h4` must be a distinct
  set (omitted headers default to `1..4`). The same profile must be set on both
  ends. One obfuscation engine per process.
- **`link_bandwidth` + `link_rtt` are a pair.** Both-or-neither, and
  **all-or-nothing across every path**: declare on every `[[paths]]` block or
  none (the shared pace is sized to the slowest declared link, undefined under a
  partial declaration). They take effect **only** under
  `scheduler.policy = "weighted"` with `pacing_enabled = true`; otherwise a
  declared bandwidth is inert. When active they are **mutually exclusive** with
  the raw `scheduler.per_path_capacity_fps` / `pacing_burst_frames` knobs —
  declare link bandwidth *or* set the frame-slot knobs, not both.
- **scheduler off-unless-present.** No `[scheduler]` block ⇒ `active-backup` and
  every weighted knob ignored. Under `weighted`, omitted knobs take the defaults
  shown; the hysteresis band requires `disengage_fraction < engage_fraction`.
- **fec off-unless-present.** No `[fec]` block (or `enabled = false`) ⇒ no
  parity on the wire. `adaptive = true` requires `enabled = true`. In adaptive
  mode set **either** `target_residual` (recommended, `(0,1)`) **or**
  `safety_factor` (`>= 1`), never both; both must stay 0 (unset) in fixed mode.
- **metrics loopback-only.** `[metrics] listen` must be a loopback address (or a
  hostname resolving entirely to loopback); a non-loopback bind is refused when
  the endpoint starts. Omit the block to serve no metrics at all.

## 4. systemd units

Unit files live in `packaging/systemd/`:

```sh
cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service /etc/systemd/system/          # edge box
cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-concentrator.service /etc/systemd/system/  # VPS
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable --now wanbond-edge      # or wanbond-concentrator
```

- `systemctl reload wanbond-<role>` sends SIGHUP: the daemon re-reads the
  config and applies the **path diff** (add/remove uplinks) without tearing
  down the live tunnel. A bad reload is logged and the running config kept.
- `Restart=on-failure` restarts the daemon if the tunnel engine tears down
  unexpectedly (the process exits non-zero in that case by design).
- The units run with `CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN` (TUN creation) plus
  standard hardening. If you set `wireguard.listen_port` below 1024, add
  `CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE` to the bounding set.

### Interface addressing and routing (operator-owned)

The daemon creates the TUN interface (`wanbond0`) and brings it
administratively **UP** itself (`SIOCSIFFLAGS`/`IFF_UP`) right after creation —
so a write to it never silently fails with `EIO` for want of a link-up step —
but owns ONLY the tunnel engine: **it never assigns addresses or routes**
(wg-quick style, no privileged shell-outs). Assign them with a
**systemd-networkd `.network` file**, using the inner addresses from
`allowed_ips`:

```ini
# /etc/systemd/network/10-wanbond0.network  (edge; concentrator: 10.77.0.1/24)
[Match]
Name=wanbond0

[Network]
Address=10.77.0.2/24
```

```sh
systemctl enable --now systemd-networkd
```

Do **not** use a `[Service] ExecStartPost=ip address add … dev wanbond0`
drop-in: the units are `Type=exec`, so systemd considers the service started
the instant `execve()` returns — *before* the daemon has created `wanbond0` —
and the `ExecStartPost` would run against a not-yet-existent interface, fail
with "Cannot find device", and (being an un-prefixed `ExecStartPost`) fail the
unit and trip the `Restart=on-failure` crash-loop. networkd is race-free: it
watches for the interface and applies the address the moment `wanbond0`
appears, whenever that is. (The daemon exposes no `sd_notify` readiness, so
there is no ordering guarantee to hang an `ExecStartPost` on.)

## 5. Firewall

### Concentrator: UDP listen port

Allow the WireGuard UDP port in from the WAN (and, on OCI, in the subnet's
security list / NSG as well — both layers must pass):

```sh
iptables -I INPUT -p udp --dport 51820 -j ACCEPT
```

### Concentrator: tunnel-interface ACCEPT — REQUIRED (OCI default-REJECT caveat)

The concentrator **must ACCEPT traffic arriving on the wanbond tunnel
interface, ordered ahead of any default REJECT rule**:

```sh
iptables -I INPUT -i wanbond0 -j ACCEPT
```

Why this is required: Oracle Cloud (OCI) images ship an INPUT chain ending in

```
-A INPUT -j REJECT --reject-with icmp-host-prohibited
```

which silently applies to the tunnel interface too. ICMP echo is allowed by an
earlier rule, so **ping through the tunnel works while any TCP connection
through the tunnel fails with a confusing `No route to host`** (the REJECT's
ICMP host-prohibited answer). This exact failure was hit during P0 real-host
testing. `iptables -I` (insert at head) places the ACCEPT ahead of the appended
(`-A`) REJECT; verify the ordering with `iptables -S INPUT` — the tunnel ACCEPT
must appear before the `-j REJECT` line. The same applies to any distro or
provider whose default firewall ends INPUT with REJECT/DROP (e.g. firewalld
zones): treat "tunnel ACCEPT ahead of the default reject" as a required
concentrator deployment step.

### Concentrator: persist the rules across reboots — REQUIRED

`iptables -I` mutates only the **runtime** chain. On OCI Ubuntu the
`netfilter-persistent` boot service **restores `/etc/iptables/rules.v4`**, so a
reboot silently discards any runtime-only rule and the tunnel ACCEPT (and the
UDP-port ACCEPT) revert to the default REJECT with no signal until
re-provisioned (defect D7). The rules **must** therefore be written to the boot
rules file. On Debian/Ubuntu:

```sh
sudo apt-get install -y iptables-persistent   # provides netfilter-persistent
sudo netfilter-persistent save                # snapshots the runtime chain to /etc/iptables/rules.v4
```

(`service iptables save`, or a firewalld permanent rule, on EL.) Verify the
tunnel ACCEPT survives by confirming it appears in the saved file:

```sh
sudo grep -- '-A INPUT -i wanbond0 -j ACCEPT' /etc/iptables/rules.v4
```

The rule references the tunnel interface by name, so it only matches while
`wanbond0` exists; restoring it at boot (before the daemon starts) is harmless.

This save is **idempotent** — re-running it against an unchanged chain is a
no-op — and the real-host provisioning (`test/realhosts`,
`Provision`/`TestRealProvision`, run via `just realhosts-provision`) performs
and asserts exactly these steps automatically: it installs
`iptables-persistent`, inserts the runtime rule, runs `netfilter-persistent
save`, and re-inspects that the rule is present in `/etc/iptables/rules.v4`.

### Edge

Outbound UDP to the concentrator's `endpoint` (and any per-path `dest_addr`)
must be open on every uplink; no inbound rules are needed — the edge initiates.

## 6. Observability

Each daemon serves Prometheus metrics on the loopback-only `[metrics] listen`
address (`curl -s http://127.0.0.1:9090/metrics`). Logs go to stderr →
`journalctl -u wanbond-<role>`.

## 7. MTU

The daemon sets the TUN MTU itself from the bonded-overhead budget (see
`docs/p1-mtu.md`); do not override it. If an on-path MTU below the default
1500 is in play, see the TCP MSS-clamp guidance in that document.

## 8. Limitations

### UDP-blocking networks defeat wanbond (no TCP/TLS fallback — by design)

wanbond carries every path over **UDP** (WireGuard's transport). Its
DPI-resistance goal is that a network which *inspects* traffic cannot
distinguish the flow from ordinary UDP: the outer frame codec (amnezia
obfuscation + the FEC-framed Bind) removes the WireGuard/VPN fingerprint, so a
